content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Mohammad Rahmani on UnsplashWhen I first started learning Python, I had a very simple idea of what “good code” looked like.
I thought good code meant complex code.
Large projects.
Advanced algorithms.
Sophisticated systems.
The kind of code that looked impressive when someone else saw it.
At least that’s what I believed for a long time.
What I didn’t understand back then was that some of the best code you’ll ever write doesn’t feel impressive at all.
In fact…
The best code often becomes invisible.
It runs quietly.
It solves problems consistently.
It becomes so reliable that eventually…
You stop thinking about it completely.
And one small Python automation script taught me that lesson better than any tutorial ever had.
Because after building that script, something strange happened.
At first, I kept checking whether it was running properly.
Then slowly…
